planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into containers or beds in full sun.

directions: Set nursery plants into rich, well-drained soil and water them in thoroughly. Let the cascading stems spill from baskets and window boxes, shearing lightly if they thin. Grown from cuttings rather than seed, these vegetative types bloom heavily all summer in full sun.

planting.note: Set hardened transplants out in early spring while nights are still cool, or in early autumn for fall color.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and barely cover, keeping the flat cool and dark until sprouts appear in one to two weeks. Grow on in bright, cool conditions and pinch nothing; these produce very large blotched faces up to four inches across. Feed lightly every couple of weeks and deadhead spent blooms to keep the plant flowering through cool spring and fall weather.

planting.note: Transplant in cool spring soil or in fall; this series tolerates both heat and cold better than most.

directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before your target set-out date, covering only lightly. Move seedlings to individual cells once true leaves form and grow them cool and bright to prevent stretching. Plant out at the same depth they grew and shear back midseason if blooms slow, watering steadily but never letting the crown sit soggy.

planting.note: Set out in early spring or fall; the upright, uniform habit holds flowers facing outward for good bedding display.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a fine, moist mix and hold at a steady cool temperature for even sprouting. Grow the seedlings hard in good light so they stay stocky, then transplant into rich, well-drained beds. Regular deadheading and a balanced feed keep the tidy plants covered in medium-sized blotched flowers from cool spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in cool spring or autumn beds where the very large flowers can be admired up close.

directions: Sow into cool, moist media and barely cover, since a little darkness aids germination. Pot up the seedlings and grow them bright and airy to build strong crowns before setting out. These carry some of the largest faces of any pansy, so give steady moisture and light feeding, and remove faded blooms to prolong the show.

planting.note: Excellent for fall planting to overwinter in mild zones, or set out in earliest spring for cascading baskets.

directions: Start seed indoors and keep it cool and evenly moist until it germinates in one to three weeks. Space plants wider than upright pansies since each one trails and mounds to fill a basket or bed edge. Water containers before they dry fully and feed regularly through the cool season; this vigorous type shrugs off frost and rebounds strongly in spring after a mild winter.

planting.note: A pansy-viola cross bred for cold hardiness; ideal for early spring or overwintering fall plantings.

directions: Sow shallowly into cool media and expect sprouts within two weeks. This cross combines the larger flower of a pansy with the toughness and bloom count of a viola, so it takes hard weather in stride. Plant into well-drained beds, keep evenly watered, and trim leggy growth to refresh the flush of medium-sized blotched flowers.

planting.note: Compact and cold-tough; well suited to fall bedding that carries through winter in mild regions.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and germinate cool for even, quick emergence. Grow the seedlings in bright light to keep them compact, then transplant into free-draining soil. The neat, weather-resistant plants bloom heavily in cool conditions; deadhead and feed lightly to keep flowers coming through spring and after mild winters.

planting.note: Set out in cool spring or fall to show off the heavily waved petal edges.

directions: Start seed indoors, covering lightly, and hold cool until seedlings appear. Grow on bright and airy so the compact plants stay sturdy. Prized for their deeply ruffled and frilled petals, they perform best in cool weather; keep the soil evenly moist and pick off spent blooms to encourage a continuous display.

planting.note: A frilled, bicolor strain best enjoyed in cool spring and autumn containers.

directions: Sow onto a moist, fine mix and barely cover, germinating at cool temperatures. Move seedlings up promptly and grow them cool to build strong plants that support the large, wavy flowers. Blooms open in soft bicolor blends with ruffled edges; deadhead often and feed steadily to extend flowering through the cool months.

planting.note: Small-flowered and very floriferous; a good choice for cold fall and early spring bedding.

directions: Germinate the fine seed cool and barely covered for even emergence. Grow seedlings bright to keep them stocky, then plant into well-drained beds. This series carries many smaller flowers rather than a few large ones and holds up to cold well; light feeding and occasional shearing keep it dense and blooming.

planting.note: A distinctive sky-blue and yellow bicolor for cool spring or fall containers and borders.

directions: Sow shallowly into a cool, moist mix and expect germination within two weeks. Grow the seedlings hard in good light so they stay compact and free-flowering. Named for its butterfly-like blue-and-yellow face with dark whiskers, this selection blooms best in cool weather; deadhead and feed lightly to keep the color coming.

planting.note: Large, softly shaded flowers that show best in cool spring weather.

directions: Start seed indoors under cool, evenly moist conditions and cover only lightly. Grow the young plants bright and airy to prevent stretch, then set them into fertile, well-drained soil. This series is known for large flowers in pastel and blended tones; keep watered, feed every couple of weeks, and remove spent blooms to lengthen the season.

planting.note: An early, large-flowered strain suited to cool spring and autumn beds.

directions: Sow onto moist media, barely cover, and germinate cool for even results. Pot up promptly and grow bright to keep plants compact and well-branched. Valued for early, generous flowering across a wide color mix; give steady moisture, light feeding, and regular deadheading to hold the display through the cool season.

planting.note: A striking orange-and-purple bicolor; plant in cool spring or fall for vivid contrast.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered, expecting sprouts within two weeks. Grow the seedlings in bright light for stocky, well-branched plants. This award-winning selection opens electric orange faces against deep violet-blue wings; keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and deadhead to sustain its bold color in cool weather.

planting.note: Faceless, clear single colors that read cleanly from a distance in spring or fall beds.

directions: Sow shallowly into cool, moist media and hold until seedlings emerge. Grow them bright to keep plants compact and productive. Unlike blotched types, these open in solid, clear colors with no dark face, giving bold blocks of color; water evenly, feed lightly, and remove spent flowers to keep the plants blooming.

planting.note: A uniform, medium-large bedding series for cool spring and autumn color.

directions: Barely cover the seed and germinate at cool temperatures for even emergence. Grow seedlings in bright, airy conditions to build sturdy plants. This dependable series flowers heavily across a broad color range; plant into well-drained soil, keep evenly moist, and deadhead regularly to prolong bloom into early summer.

planting.note: A multiflora type with many smaller flowers; well suited to overwintering fall plantings.

directions: Sow cool and barely covered for even, quick germination. Grow the seedlings bright so they stay compact and branch well. This multiflora series covers itself in numerous medium flowers rather than a few large ones and takes cold weather well; keep moist, feed lightly, and shear if growth loosens to refresh bloom.

planting.note: Clear single colors without a face; a tidy series for spring and fall bedding.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered, expecting emergence within two weeks. Grow the young plants in strong light to keep them stocky and even. Blooms open in solid, faceless colors that mass cleanly in beds and containers; water steadily, feed lightly, and remove faded flowers to keep the plants performing through cool weather.

planting.note: Large blotched faces on uniform plants for cool-season display.

directions: Sow onto moist, fine media and barely cover, germinating cool for even results. Move seedlings up and grow them bright to build strong crowns. This series shows large, clearly blotched faces across a wide color range; give fertile, well-drained soil, steady moisture, and regular deadheading to extend the flowering season.

planting.note: A vivid, faceless deep-orange selection that glows in cool spring and fall light.

directions: Sow shallowly into cool media and germinate barely covered. Grow seedlings bright and airy so they stay compact and free-flowering. Named for an orange sapphire, this selection opens pure, glowing orange flowers with no dark face; keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and deadhead to sustain the intense color through cool weather.

planting.note: Soft, shifting pastel blends of apricot, rose, and lavender for cool-season color.

directions: Germinate the fine seed cool and barely covered for even emergence. Grow the seedlings bright to keep plants stocky and well-branched. Flowers open in muted, blended pastels that deepen and shift as temperatures change, giving an antique look; water evenly, feed lightly, and deadhead to keep the soft colors coming.

planting.note: An early, large-flowered series that colors up quickly for spring and fall beds.

directions: Sow cool and barely covered, expecting germination within two weeks. Grow the seedlings bright and airy for compact, quick-blooming plants. This series flowers early with large faces across many colors; plant into well-drained soil, keep evenly moist, and deadhead often to keep the display going through the cool season.

planting.note: A weather-tough series that holds color through cold snaps in spring or overwintered fall beds.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered for even sprouting. Grow the young plants bright to keep them compact and branching. Bred for cold tolerance and steady flowering, this series performs well in early spring and after mild winters; plant into free-draining soil, water evenly, and shear lightly if the plants loosen to refresh bloom.

planting.note: A uniform, medium-large bedding series with strong color range for cool seasons.

directions: Sow onto moist media and barely cover, germinating at cool temperatures. Grow the seedlings bright and airy so they stay sturdy and even. This dependable series produces medium-large flowers on tidy plants; give well-drained soil, steady moisture, light feeding, and regular deadheading to hold the color through spring or fall.

planting.note: Bold, richly marked faces on vigorous plants for spring and fall bedding.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered, expecting emergence within two weeks. Grow the seedlings in strong light for stocky, vigorous plants. This series offers deeply marked, richly colored faces; plant into fertile, well-drained soil, keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and deadhead to prolong the bloom into early summer.

planting.note: A cascading pansy for baskets and walls; plant in fall to overwinter or in earliest spring.

directions: Start seed indoors and keep it cool and evenly moist until it germinates in one to three weeks. Give plants room to trail, since each cascades over a basket rim or wall edge. Water containers before they fully dry and feed regularly through the cool season; this hardy trailer flowers heavily and rebounds well after light frosts.

planting.note: A semi-trailing pansy that mounds and spills; good for baskets in cool weather.

directions: Sow indoors into cool, moist media and hold until seedlings emerge. Space plants generously so their spreading habit can fill a container or bed edge. Keep evenly watered and feed regularly; the plants mound and trail with numerous medium flowers and take cool, frosty conditions in stride, blooming best in spring and fall.

planting.note: A classic heirloom strain with very large, well-blotched flowers for cool spring and fall.

directions: Sow the seed into a cool, moist mix and barely cover, allowing up to three weeks for full germination. Grow the seedlings bright and cool so they develop into strong plants. This time-honored Swiss strain bears large flowers with bold central blotches across a rich color mix; keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and deadhead to extend the bloom.

planting.note: A vivid orange-faced bicolor in the Joker group for cool-season contrast.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered, expecting sprouts within two weeks. Grow the seedlings bright for compact, well-branched plants. Like others in the Joker group, it opens bright orange faces contrasted against dark violet wings and whiskers; keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and deadhead to keep the striking color coming in cool weather.

planting.note: A very cold-tough, floriferous viola series ideal for early spring and overwintered fall beds.

directions: Sow the fine seed cool and barely covered, expecting germination within two weeks. Grow the seedlings bright to keep them compact and heavily budded. This series smothers itself in small flowers and tolerates hard cold, blooming through mild winters in temperate zones; water evenly, feed lightly, and shear midseason to renew the flush.

planting.note: A compact, early viola series that flowers heavily in cool weather and overwinters in mild zones.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered for even emergence. Grow the seedlings in bright light so they stay tight and well-branched. This early, uniform series covers itself in small flowers and stands up to cold; plant into well-drained soil, keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and trim if it loosens to keep it blooming.

planting.note: A scented, soft-yellow and lavender heirloom viola grown as a hardy cool-season perennial.

directions: Sow seed cool and barely covered, allowing up to three weeks for full germination. Grow the seedlings bright and set them into moist, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This fragrant selection bears creamy-yellow flowers edged in soft lavender; deadhead and shear after the main flush, and it will rebloom and persist for several cool seasons.

planting.note: A vanilla-scented cream-and-violet viola grown as a hardy cool-season perennial.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered, expecting slow, uneven emergence over two to three weeks. Grow the seedlings bright and plant into moist, well-drained soil. This fragrant selection opens cream flowers flecked and edged with violet; deadhead often and shear lightly after flowering to encourage rebloom and keep the plant vigorous through the cool seasons.

planting.note: A streaked purple-and-white viola grown as a hardy cool-season perennial.

directions: Sow seed cool and barely covered, allowing up to three weeks for germination. Grow the seedlings in good light and set them into moist, well-drained soil. Flowers open in a distinctive splashed and streaked pattern of purple over white, no two quite alike; deadhead and shear after the main flush to keep the plant tidy and reblooming.

planting.note: A near-black, small-flowered viola that self-sows; direct sow in cool soil spring or fall.

directions: Sow directly on prepared soil and barely cover, or start indoors and transplant young. This selection opens tiny, velvety near-black flowers with a small gold eye and reseeds gently around the garden. Keep the soil moist while establishing; it withstands frost easily and blooms in cool spring and fall, dropping seed that returns in following years.

planting.note: A whiskered, two-toned viola series with strongly marked faces for cool-season color.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered for even emergence within two weeks. Grow the seedlings bright to keep them compact and floriferous. This series opens small flowers with bold dark whiskers radiating across contrasting faces; plant into well-drained soil, keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and shear midseason to refresh the bloom in cool weather.

planting.note: A spreading, extremely cold-hardy viola series good for baskets and overwintered beds.

directions: Sow seed cool and barely covered, expecting germination within two weeks. Space the plants to allow for their spreading, mounding habit in beds or containers. This vigorous series flowers profusely and shrugs off hard cold, often surviving mild winters to rebound in spring; keep evenly watered, feed lightly, and trim if growth loosens to renew the display.

planting.note: A heavily flowering, uniform viola series for spring and fall bedding.

directions: Germinate seed cool and barely covered for even sprouting. Grow the seedlings bright so they stay compact and densely budded. This series lives up to its name with a heavy, early set of small flowers on tidy plants that take cold well; plant into well-drained soil, keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and shear midseason to keep it full.

planting.note: A finely veined golden viola with dramatic dark whiskering for cool-season interest.

directions: Sow seed cool and barely covered, expecting germination within two weeks. Grow the seedlings in strong light to keep them compact and free-flowering. This selection opens warm golden faces intricately veined with fine mahogany whiskers, giving a striking close-up pattern; keep evenly moist, feed lightly, and deadhead or shear to prolong the bloom in cool weather.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the soil has warmed and all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Press seeds about a quarter inch into warm, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching and stake tall stems if your site is windy. Cut the fully doubled chartreuse blooms for the vase just as they open, which keeps new buds forming all season.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost when nights stay mild.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in a sunny bed with good airflow to reduce mildew. Water at the base rather than overhead and deadhead spent flowers to prolong the display. Harvest the deep wine-red heads when the center petals lift away from the disk for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm garden soil after frost danger ends.

directions: Plant seeds about a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to give each plant elbow room. These vigorous, mildew-tolerant plants carry large double blooms in a wide color range on sturdy stems. Snip flowers regularly or leave a few to feed goldfinches and pollinators in late summer.

planting.note: Wait until the soil is warm, then sow in place after the last frost.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in a bright, open spot and keep moist through germination. This heirloom strain produces broad, semi-double to double flowers in mixed pastel and bright shades on long stems ideal for cutting. Deadhead often and harvest blooms in the morning for the freshest bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow once frost has passed and the ground feels warm to the touch.

directions: Cover seeds with a quarter inch of soil and firm gently so they contact moisture. The shaggy, quill-petaled blooms open in a mix of warm and cool tones on tall branching plants. Cut stems just as the flowers open fully to enjoy the twisted, spidery petals in arrangements.

planting.note: Sow in place after the last frost date for your area.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and thin to prevent crowding. The unusual chartreuse-green semi-double blooms are prized by florists for their fresh, foliage-like color. Deadhead spent heads and cut fresh flowers frequently to keep the plant producing.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier for a head start.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in warm, fertile soil and keep the bed evenly moist until sprouting. This florist favorite blends dusky red centers with lime-green outer petals, and each plant varies subtly in color. Harvest when blooms are about three-quarters open for strong stems and long vase life.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once soil warms past the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds shallowly and give plants an open, sunny spot with good airflow. The muted orange blooms shade to soft green and coral, making unusual and popular cut flowers. Pinch early growth for more stems and cut blooms as they open to keep production going.

planting.note: Direct sow after the frost-free date in warm soil.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in rich soil and thin seedlings to reduce disease pressure. Blooms open in soft blush-pink washed with lime green, a sought-after wedding and bouquet color. Deadhead regularly and harvest in the cool morning hours for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep and keep evenly moist through sprouting. This cut-flower series bears smaller, uniform double blooms on wiry stems with excellent mildew resistance. Snip flowers freely, as frequent cutting keeps the plants branching and blooming into fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Cover seed with about a quarter inch of soil and thin young plants for airflow. The tidy pink double flowers hold their form well and resist splitting in wet weather. Cut stems just as blooms open and remove faded heads to encourage continuous flowering.

planting.note: Sow in place once frost has passed and soil is warm.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Many flowers form a raised scabiosa-like crest of tufted petals in soft peach, though a portion bloom single. Harvest the crested blooms as they open and deadhead the rest to keep the display fresh.

planting.note: Direct sow after the frost-free date into warm ground.

directions: Set seeds shallowly and keep the soil moist until seedlings emerge. The crested, pincushion-style blooms open in soft lilac and lavender tones loved for cottage bouquets. Cut fully formed flowers for the vase and remove spent heads to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost or start indoors for early color.

directions: Scatter seeds a quarter inch deep and thin to a few inches apart for edging or containers. These dwarf plants carry small semi-double to double flowers in mixed colors and rebloom without deadheading. Pinch once when young for a fuller mound, then simply enjoy the compact summer-long color.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings so plants can bush out. This old pompon strain produces small, tightly rounded button flowers in a bright color mix. Cut the neat blooms for petite arrangements and deadhead to keep new buttons coming.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost when the soil warms.

directions: Cover seeds lightly and keep moist until germination in a sunny bed. Compact plants bear rounded, fully double pompon blooms in a cheerful assortment of colors. Snip flowers regularly for small posies and remove faded heads to extend the season.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost in warm soil.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep and thin for airflow to limit mildew. The double blooms are speckled and striped in contrasting colors, so no two flowers look quite alike. Cut the flashy heads for bouquets and deadhead to keep the show going into fall.

planting.note: Sow directly outdoors once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and keep evenly moist to start. Bicolor double blooms are striped and flecked in candy shades on branching plants. Harvest flowers as they open and remove old heads to encourage a long bloom season.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost date.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep and thin plants for good air movement. Large, fully double flowers open in pure creamy white on tall, strong stems for cutting. Deadhead spent blooms and harvest fresh ones in the morning for bright white bouquets.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Cover seeds with a quarter inch of soil and keep moist through sprouting. Big, dahlia-form flowers open in rich deep rose-purple atop tall, branching stems. Cut blooms when petals fully reflex and deadhead to keep new flowers forming.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in a warm, sunny site.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings for airflow. Large double flowers open in a clear, soft yellow on tall stems suited to the back of a border. Snip blooms for the vase and deadhead often to keep the plants productive.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost, or start indoors for earlier bloom.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. This award-winning variety carries very large, vivid rose-pink double blooms on strong stems. Cut freely for arrangements and remove faded flowers to keep buds coming through fall.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after all frost has passed.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep and thin for good air circulation. Broad, flat, dahlia-like double blooms open in a rich mix of colors on tall stems. Harvest the large flowers as they open and deadhead to encourage continued flowering.

planting.note: Sow in place after the last frost for a long cutting season.

directions: Cover seeds with a quarter inch of soil and keep evenly moist to germinate. Bred for cutting, these branching plants produce medium double blooms on long stems in mixed colors. The more you cut, the more they flower, so harvest often through the summer.

planting.note: Sow after frost or set out transplants once nights are mild.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and average, well-drained soil. This tough interspecific hybrid forms a mounded, disease-resistant plant covered in single cherry-rose flowers. It self-cleans and needs no deadheading, blooming nonstop from summer until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or transplant into warm soil.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in a sunny spot and water sparingly once established. These low, spreading plants shrug off heat, humidity, and mildew while blooming in bright orange. No deadheading is needed as the plants self-clean and flower steadily until frost.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost or plant out young transplants.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun with good drainage. This durable hybrid forms neat mounds of single white daisy-like flowers that resist disease. It requires no deadheading and blooms continuously from early summer through the first frost.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and average soil. This award-winning selection bears fully double, rounded blooms in hot cherry-red on a compact, mildew-resistant mound. The flowers hold their color well and need no deadheading to keep blooming until frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost or set out transplants once soil is warm.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun with good drainage. This heat- and disease-tolerant hybrid carries double orange-red blooms on a tidy, compact plant. It self-cleans and flowers nonstop through summer heat without any deadheading.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. The single white flowers are striped with rose pink, giving a two-tone pinwheel effect all summer. This compact, mildew-resistant hybrid self-cleans and blooms until the first frost.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost or plant out transplants.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and light, well-drained soil. Bright single yellow blooms cover the compact, heat-loving mounds all season long. The plants resist common leaf diseases and need no deadheading to keep flowering.

planting.note: Sow after frost once the soil has warmed thoroughly.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. This tough species zinnia forms a low, spreading mat of narrow leaves and small single orange flowers. Highly drought and disease tolerant, it self-cleans and blooms from summer to frost.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Set seeds a quarter inch deep in a sunny spot with sharp drainage. Small single white flowers with golden centers cover the low, mounding plants nonstop. This heat-loving, mildew-resistant zinnia thrives in beds, edges, and containers without deadheading.

planting.note: Sow in place after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and average soil. This heirloom Mexican zinnia bears small bicolor double flowers in warm mahogany, gold, and orange tones. Drought tolerant and free-flowering, it makes charming small bouquets all summer.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Cover seeds with a quarter inch of soil and keep lightly moist to germinate. The small, richly patterned flowers blend russet, bronze, and gold on compact, tidy plants. This drought-tolerant Mexican zinnia blooms freely and suits edging and cutting alike.

planting.note: Sow directly after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. This compact heirloom bears semi-double flowers in mahogany-red tipped with gold. It tolerates heat and drought well and blooms steadily from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost, or set out plants in spring; established clumps are cold hardy.

directions: Plant seeds shallowly in gritty, fast-draining soil and be patient, as germination is slow and uneven. This native perennial forms a low, spreading mat topped with small golden-yellow daisies. Once established it is extremely drought tolerant and blooms through the hottest part of summer.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring after frost in a hot, dry spot.

directions: Plant seeds shallowly in sharply drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun. This small native subshrub forms a tidy mound of narrow leaves topped with creamy white flowers. Give it little water once established, and it will bloom on and off from spring into fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep in full sun and average, well-drained soil. This wild species bears small single flowers in brick red or gold that dry to papery seed heads. Easy and drought tolerant, it reseeds readily and gives a naturalistic cottage look.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in mid to late autumn once soil has cooled.

directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up about three times as deep as the bulb is tall in well-drained soil. This vigorous Darwin Hybrid returns reliably for several seasons and stands up well to wind. Expect large scarlet cups on tall stems in mid-spring; let the foliage yellow fully before removing it.

planting.note: Plant in autumn several weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Dig a hole roughly six to eight inches deep and set bulbs with the tip upward. A golden Darwin Hybrid sport, it opens into broad buttery-yellow goblets that catch the light. Water in at planting, then keep the bed on the dry side once flowering finishes.

planting.note: Best planted in cool autumn soil for strong spring roots.

directions: Bury each bulb six to eight inches deep in loose, fertile ground. This Darwin Hybrid carries very large rose-pink blooms on sturdy stems that resist spring storms. It naturalizes better than most large tulips, so leave clumps undisturbed to bloom again.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny, sheltered bed.

directions: Set bulbs about seven inches deep, spaced a hand's width apart. This Darwin Hybrid shows glowing orange-red petals edged in a fine yellow band. Cut a few for the vase just as the buds show color, and leave the rest to fade naturally.

planting.note: Plant in autumn once nights turn cold.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in full sun with sharp drainage. Blooms open soft yellow and warm to apricot-orange as the sun ages them. Deadhead spent flowers but keep the leaves until they wither to feed next year's bulb.

planting.note: Plant in mid-autumn before the ground hardens.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep and a hand's width apart. This Triumph tulip offers deep beetroot-purple blooms that pair well with lighter spring flowers. Grow in full sun and lift or replant if flowering weakens after a few years.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Plant bulbs five to six inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. This shorter Triumph shows soft orange petals brushed with purple flames, and the flowers carry a light scent. It works well near the front of a border or in pots.

planting.note: Set out in autumn as soil temperatures drop.

directions: Plant six inches deep with the pointed end up in loose soil. This Triumph produces bright magenta-pink cups on strong stems ideal for cutting. Harvest stems for the vase when the color shows but buds are still closed for the longest display.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in full sun and good drainage.

directions: Dig in bulbs about six inches deep and space evenly for a full clump. This Triumph shows deep maroon petals edged with a broad butter-yellow margin. Its bold two-tone flowers stand out at the border edge in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early spring bloom.

directions: Set bulbs five to six inches deep in well-drained soil. A Single Early tulip, it opens soft salmon-apricot flowers that carry a gentle fragrance early in the season. Plant in a sheltered spot so the early blooms are protected from harsh wind.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep and space them evenly. This Single Early tulip produces cheerful cherry-pink cups among the first tulips to open. It also forces well in pots for early indoor color.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for late spring flowering.

directions: Bury bulbs six to eight inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. This Single Late tulip is famous for its deep maroon-black satin cups on tall stems. Pair it with white or pale tulips for contrast, and let the leaves ripen fully after bloom.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny border.

directions: Plant seven to eight inches deep in fertile ground with good drainage. This tall Single Late tulip opens rose-pink petals with apricot-orange edges and a warm interior. Its long stems make it a favorite for large spring arrangements.

planting.note: Set out in autumn once soil cools.

directions: Plant bulbs about seven inches deep in loose, well-drained soil. This Single Late tulip carries large ivory-white oval blooms on very tall stems. Grow in full sun and give it shelter from strong wind to protect the tall flowers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set bulbs six to eight inches deep with the tip pointing up. This Single Late tulip holds clear rose-pink cups that glow in late spring light. It naturalizes reasonably well, so leave clumps in place for repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early to mid-spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in well-drained ground. This Fosteriana, also called White Emperor, produces large creamy-white flowers early in the season. It is one of the more reliably perennial tulips, returning for several years in a settled spot.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep and a hand's width apart. This Fosteriana opens large carrot-orange cups with a yellow base early in spring. Its big, bright flowers show up well from a distance in a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant in autumn before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in freely draining soil. Also sold as Red Emperor, this Fosteriana carries huge fiery-red blooms among the earliest large tulips. Give it full sun and it will make a bold early-spring statement.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn near the front of a border.

directions: Set bulbs about five inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. This Greigii tulip is short with bright scarlet cups above leaves marked with maroon streaks. The mottled foliage adds interest even before the flowers open, and it suits rockeries and edges.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in full sun.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep and space evenly. This Greigii is multi-flowering, sending up several coral-red blooms from each bulb. The clustered flowers over striped foliage make a full show at the front of a bed.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a well-drained rockery or edge.

directions: Set bulbs four to five inches deep in sharply drained soil. This Kaufmanniana, or water-lily tulip, opens flat pale-pink and white star flowers with a red ring inside. It blooms very early and is well suited to rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep near the front of a bed. This Kaufmanniana shows carmine-red outer petals opening to pale rose and cream inside. The early flowers spread wide in sun and the foliage carries faint mottling.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a hot, dry, sunny spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in fast-draining ground. This golden form of the lady tulip has yellow petals flushed red or bronze on the reverse. It thrives in warm gardens and naturalizes where summers are dry.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny rock garden.

directions: Set the small bulbs three to four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Each bulb produces a cluster of star-shaped yellow flowers with white tips that open flat in sun. This tough species naturalizes freely and is ideal for rockeries and gravel.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a warm, well-drained site.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in sharp-draining soil. This early species carries several small cream-white flowers with a yellow center on each stem. It blooms very early, naturalizes well, and copes with lean, dry ground.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in sun or light shade.

directions: Set bulbs four to five inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. This species bears nodding yellow flowers that are lightly scented and lift their heads as they open. It tolerates part shade and can naturalize in grass or under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep and space to allow clumps to form. This multi-flowering species tulip sends up four to six orange-red blooms per stem. It is reliably perennial and naturalizes well in sunny borders and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in gritty soil.

directions: Set the small bulbs about four inches deep in sharply drained soil. This dwarf species tulip opens soft sulphur-yellow flowers flushed apricot-orange. It is perfect for rock gardens and troughs and returns dependably in dry, sunny spots.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a hot, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in lean, gritty soil. This low species tulip bears brilliant scarlet flowers with a dark center and narrow gray-green leaves. It thrives in dry, baking sites and suits the front of a rockery.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in a warm, sunny position. This Cretan species tulip opens lilac-pink flowers with a bright yellow base. It spreads by runners in dry, sunny sites and is well suited to milder gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sheltered, sunny bed.

directions: Set bulbs six to eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This Parrot tulip has ruffled, fringed dark maroon-black petals that twist and curl. Plant it in a spot sheltered from wind, since the large heads can be top-heavy.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sheltered spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in rich, freely draining soil. This Parrot tulip carries deep crimson-red blooms with heavily fringed, curled petals and green feathering. Grow in a wind-protected site so the showy flowers hold their form.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny, sheltered bed.

directions: Set bulbs six to eight inches deep in well-drained ground. This Parrot tulip has large primrose-yellow blooms brushed with crimson flames and ruffled edges. Its big, dramatic flowers make excellent cut stems for the vase.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for early spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This Double Early tulip opens full, peony-like sulphur-yellow flowers with a light fragrance. Its low, sturdy stems suit pots and the front of the border.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs about five inches deep and space evenly. This Double Early tulip produces dense, deep red peony-form blooms early in the season. Its short, strong stems hold the heavy flowers well in pots and beds.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This Double Late tulip bears soft pink peony-like flowers that pale toward the edges. The full ruffled blooms are lightly scented and make lovely cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep with the tip pointing up. This Double Late tulip opens full, pure-white peony-form flowers late in the season. Plant in a sheltered spot so rain does not weigh down the heavy double heads.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a sunny border.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground. This Double Late tulip carries dense violet-purple peony-form blooms despite its name. The full flowers last well and add rich late-spring color to the border.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in well-drained soil. This Lily-flowered tulip has slender, pointed orange petals that reflex outward and carry a light scent. Its graceful, tapered blooms on tall stems are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground. This Lily-flowered tulip bears elegant pure-white blooms with pointed, reflexing petals on tall stems. Its clean lines make it a classic choice for formal borders and vases.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in well-drained soil. This Lily-flowered tulip has bright primrose-yellow petals that taper to sharp, gracefully reflexed points. The narrow, star-like blooms stand out in a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep with the pointed end up. This Lily-flowered tulip carries clear rose-pink blooms with a white base and slender, reflexed petals. The elegant shape and soft color suit both borders and cutting gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This Viridiflora tulip has ivory-white petals with a broad green feathered stripe up each one. The subtle green markings make it a long-lasting, refined choice for the border.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs six to eight inches deep in well-drained soil. This Fringed tulip has rich wine-red blooms edged with a delicate crystalline fringe. The finely cut petal margins give the tall flowers a lacy, textured look.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn several weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Set bulbs about three times their own depth with the pointed nose facing up, in well-drained soil. Leave the foliage standing until it yellows so the bulb can rebuild for next year. Expect large solid-yellow trumpet blooms in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall so roots establish before hard frost.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly six inches deep in loose, freely draining ground. Blooms open pale creamy-yellow and fade to near white as they age. Cut spent flowers but keep the leaves until they wither naturally.

planting.note: Autumn-plant into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Bury bulbs about three bulb-heights deep in a spot with good drainage. The sulphur-yellow trumpet lightens toward white from the throat outward as the flower matures. Feed lightly after bloom and let the foliage ripen fully.

planting.note: Plant in fall before the soil freezes.

directions: Dig holes about six inches deep and set bulbs firmly with the tip upward. Flowers open with a lemon cup against white petals, the cup fading to cream. A dependable, vigorous naturalizer for borders and lawns.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits this widely grown variety.

directions: Plant bulbs three times as deep as they are tall in well-drained ground. Soft-yellow flowers carry a faint vanilla fragrance and hold up well as cut stems. One of the best daffodils for large naturalized sweeps.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for mid-spring color.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in soil that drains freely. White petals frame a ruffled cup that opens apricot-pink and deepens with sun. Remove faded blooms and allow foliage to ripen before tidying.

planting.note: Fall-plant into a sunny border.

directions: Plant nose-up at about three times the bulb's depth in loose soil. White petals surround a short yellow cup rimmed with orange-red. A strong grower that returns reliably in beds and grass.

planting.note: Plant in autumn before frost sets in.

directions: Dig in bulbs about six inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Broad white petals set off a flat, frilled cup of vivid orange. The bold contrast makes it a standout in spring borders and vases.

planting.note: Autumn-plant this tall, large-flowered variety.

directions: Plant the sizable bulbs six to seven inches deep with room to clump. Large golden petals carry a bright orange cup on sturdy stems. Its height and vigor suit the back of a bulb planting.

planting.note: Plant in fall for spring bloom.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in a bright, well-drained spot. White petals cradle a cup banded coral-pink at the rim over a pale center. Deadhead once faded and let the leaves feed the bulb.

planting.note: Plant in autumn as an early bloomer.

directions: Plant bulbs about three times their depth in soil that never stays wet. White petals surround a small flat cup of orange over a paler ground. An early, free-flowering pick for edging and naturalizing.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; the heavy heads may want staking.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in a sheltered, freely draining spot. The full double flowers layer yellow petals with orange segments. Because the blooms are weighty, plant out of strong wind or provide light support.

planting.note: Fall-plant this fragrant, multi-headed variety.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Each stem carries several sweetly scented white double flowers late in the season. Excellent for cutting and for extending the daffodil display.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for late-spring fragrance.

directions: Dig bulbs in about six inches deep where drainage is good. Clustered soft-yellow double flowers bring strong perfume at the tail of the season. A fine companion to its white-flowered sport in mixed plantings.

planting.note: Plant in fall; good for forcing indoors too.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep outdoors, or pot shallowly for indoor forcing. Multiple creamy-white double flowers marked with soft apricot open per stem. Their rich scent carries well in a cut arrangement.

planting.note: Fall-plant this dwarf in the front of a bed or rockery.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three to four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. The shaggy, spiky yellow-green double flowers look almost dandelion-like. Ideal for rock gardens, edges, and containers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for scented late blooms.

directions: Bury bulbs about six inches deep in free-draining, fertile ground. Clusters of fragrant white double flowers are flecked with orange segments. A reliable, sweetly perfumed choice for late-spring cutting.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a well-drained border.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly six inches deep with the nose upright. The full double flowers show white outer petals with ruffled pink-apricot centers. Site out of heavy wind so the substantial heads stay upright.

planting.note: Autumn-plant for large double spring flowers.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in soil that drains well. Big, softly fragrant double flowers mix white petals with pale-yellow inner segments. Provide shelter or light support for the weighty blooms.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; naturalizes gracefully.

directions: Plant bulbs four to six inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil. Each stem nods with two or more pure-white, slightly reflexed flowers. This graceful heirloom is lightly scented and long-lasting in the garden.

planting.note: Fall-plant this dainty miniature in drifts.

directions: Set the small bulbs three to four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Wiry stems each carry several tiny nodding lemon-yellow bells. Perfect for rock gardens, troughs, and the front of borders.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early to mid spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in soil that drains freely. Slim stems bear one or two pure-white flowers with gracefully swept-back petals. Its delicate look suits woodland edges and mixed bulb beds.

planting.note: Plant in fall; superb in pots and edging.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three to four inches deep in well-drained soil or containers. Each short stem usually carries one to three cheerful golden-yellow flowers very early in spring. This is among the most dependable dwarf daffodils for forcing and borders.

planting.note: Fall-plant this very early bloomer.

directions: Set bulbs four to five inches deep in freely draining ground. Golden flowers with slightly swept-back petals open among the earliest of the season. It naturalizes readily and returns dependably year after year.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for bold early color.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in a sunny, well-drained spot. Yellow reflexed petals set off a long orange trumpet that intensifies with age. A vigorous, weather-tough dwarf ideal for pots and front borders.

planting.note: Fall-plant this long-trumpeted early type.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in ground that drains well. Deep-yellow flowers have strongly reflexed petals and a notably long, narrow trumpet. An early, weatherproof variety that naturalizes nicely.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for a fragrant jonquil.

directions: Set bulbs about five inches deep in well-drained, sunny ground. Rich golden flowers carry a strong, sweet fragrance on slender stems. A long-lived, reliable jonquil that is excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Fall-plant for fragrant multi-headed stems.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep where drainage is good. Each stem bears two or more scented flowers with yellow petals and a flat orange cup. The strong perfume and multiple blooms make it a favorite for arrangements.

planting.note: Fall-plant this late, dainty miniature.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three to four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Slim stems bear tiny, rounded yellow flowers with a light fragrance late in the season. Ideal for rock gardens, containers, and border edges.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for a reverse-bicolor jonquil.

directions: Set bulbs about five inches deep in well-drained, sunny soil. Lemon-yellow petals surround a cup that fades to creamy white as the flower matures. Fragrant and free-flowering, with more than one bloom per stem.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for clustered fragrant blooms.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in well-drained, sheltered ground. Each stem holds a cluster of small white flowers with bright orange cups and heady scent. A late-season tazetta that is superb for cutting.

planting.note: Fall-plant this dwarf multi-flowered tazetta.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three to four inches deep in freely draining soil. Short stems carry several dainty flowers with pale petals and soft-yellow cups. Lightly fragrant and well suited to pots and rockeries.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild zones, or force indoors elsewhere.

directions: In warm regions plant bulbs about five inches deep in well-drained soil; in colder areas force them in shallow pots of gravel and water. Stems bear clusters of fragrant golden flowers with orange cups. Unlike hardier daffodils, these tender bulbs need no cold chilling to bloom.

planting.note: Force indoors in fall and winter; plant outdoors only in frost-free zones.

directions: Nestle bulbs shoulder-to-shoulder in a shallow dish of pebbles and keep water just at the base plate, or plant outdoors in mild climates. No chilling period is needed, and stems of intensely fragrant white flowers open within a few weeks indoors. Give bright light and cool rooms to keep the stems from stretching.

planting.note: Fall-plant this fragrant late bloomer.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in moisture-retentive but well-drained soil. Broad white petals surround a tiny flat cup of yellow edged with red. Strongly scented and one of the last daffodils to bloom, it naturalizes well in grass.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for a very late, fragrant species.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in ground that stays moist through spring but drains in summer. Swept-back white petals frame a small red-rimmed yellow eye, with a sharp sweet fragrance. This old naturalizing species is among the very last daffodils to flower.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for open, split-cup flowers.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in well-drained soil. The split cup lies flat against white petals and opens pale yellow before fading to cream. Its butterfly-like form gives a distinctive look in beds and vases.

planting.note: Fall-plant this ruffled split-corona type.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in a bright, freely draining spot. White petals back a frilly split corona washed in orange. The ruffled, open face gives a bold, almost orchid-like effect.

planting.note: Plant in autumn to naturalize in grass or woodland.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep in humus-rich soil under light shade. Pale-yellow petals surround a slightly deeper, forward-thrusting trumpet. This is the classic wild daffodil, excellent for naturalizing in meadows and woodland edges.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a cool, moist, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three inches deep in acidic, humus-rich soil that stays moist in spring. The narrow golden trumpet is set behind sharply swept-back petals, like a tiny cyclamen. It thrives in cool, damp spots and self-sows where happy.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for intensely fragrant species blooms.

directions: Set bulbs about four inches deep in a warm, well-drained site. Slim rush-like leaves accompany clusters of small, deeply fragrant golden flowers. This heat-tolerant species is a fine naturalizer in southern gardens.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a cool, well-drained pocket.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three inches deep in gritty, humus-rich soil. Wiry stems nod with several small, creamy-white bell flowers with reflexed petals. It prefers cool, lightly shaded spots and looks charming in a rock garden.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild-winter regions.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in warm, well-drained soil where winters stay mild. Each stem carries a cluster of small white-petaled flowers with pale-yellow cups and strong fragrance. This tender species suits the South and West rather than cold climates.

planting.note: Fall-plant this classic golden trumpet.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Large all-golden flowers with a bold trumpet appear in early to mid spring. This historic variety is a longtime standard for naturalizing in big drifts.

planting.note: Plant in fall for a pure white spring show.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil where they will not sit wet in summer. This large-flowered white selection naturalizes well in lawns and under deciduous trees. Snowy chalices with faint purple bases open on the first warm days of spring.

planting.note: Set corms in autumn for glossy violet spring blooms.

directions: Bury corms about three inches deep and space them a couple of inches apart in a sunny bed or lawn. The rounded blooms are a deep silvery violet with a bright orange stigma at the center. Flowers appear in early to mid spring and multiply steadily over the years.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall before the first hard freeze.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in ordinary garden soil with good drainage. This vigorous purple selection has large, weather-resistant flowers that hold up well in wind and rain. Expect broad amethyst goblets in mid spring that open wide in full sun.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for striped spring flowers.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep, pointed end up, in a sunny well-drained spot. The pale petals are feathered with distinct lilac and dark purple stripes that look striking in a mass planting. Blooms open in mid spring and close again in the evening and cloudy weather.

planting.note: Set corms in fall for lavender-blue spring color.

directions: Bury corms roughly three inches deep in gritty soil and water once at planting. This selection carries large soft lavender-blue flowers with a subtle darker sheen. It naturalizes readily and lights up the garden in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for feathered spring blooms.

directions: Set corms three inches deep in free-draining soil in sun or light shade. The silvery lilac petals are heavily veined with deep purple pinstriping. Large goblet blooms appear in mid spring and are excellent for naturalizing in turf.

planting.note: Plant in fall; one of the earliest large crocus to bloom.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in a sheltered sunny site. Prized for blooming a week or two ahead of most large crocus, it opens soft silvery-blue flowers with silvery-grey outer petals. Give it good drainage and it will return reliably each late winter to early spring.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for very early spring flowers.

directions: Set the small corms two to three inches deep in sharply drained soil. This species blooms earlier and is daintier than the Dutch crocus, often pushing through late snow. Plant in generous clumps since the fine-textured flowers make their impact in numbers.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for creamy early spring blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty soil that stays dry in summer. The rounded soft creamy-yellow flowers have a deeper gold throat and are among the earliest to open. Bees visit them eagerly on the first mild days of the season.

planting.note: Set corms in autumn for pale blue early blooms.

directions: Set corms about two inches deep in a sunny, well-drained pocket or rock garden. The slender flowers are soft silvery-blue outside and paler within with a bronze base and orange stigma. They open flat in sunshine early in the spring and naturalize freely.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for bicolor early blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in full sun and sharp drainage. The buds are rich deep purple on the outside and open to reveal a clean white interior, a striking two-tone effect. Flowers appear in very early spring and are lovely in rock gardens and containers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for fragrant white early blooms.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil. The lightly fragrant white flowers are feathered lilac-grey on the outside and carry a warm yellow throat. They open in early spring and are a favorite of early-flying pollinators.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for bicolored early flowers.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed. The flowers are yellow within and washed violet on the reverse, giving a shifting color effect as they open and close. They flower very early and multiply into cheerful clumps.

planting.note: Set corms in autumn for white blooms with purple blotches.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun. The white flowers each carry a bold deep purple teardrop blotch on the outer petals. Compact and early, it is ideal for the front of borders, troughs, and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for golden blooms with bronze reverse.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in sharp-draining soil and full sun. The golden-yellow flowers are heavily stained bronze-purple on the outside, so buds look bronze until they open. It is one of the showiest snow crocus for early color.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for feathered yellow blooms.

directions: Set corms about two inches deep in gritty soil with full exposure to sun. The bright yellow flowers are boldly feathered and striped with maroon-bronze on the outer petals. They open early and reliably return and increase over the years.

planting.note: Plant in fall for soft yellow early blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in a sunny well-drained position. The rounded flowers are a gentle primrose to soft butter yellow, paler than most snow crocus. They open in early spring and pair beautifully with blue and white selections.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for pure white early flowers.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in free-draining soil in full sun. This selection offers clean pure white flowers with a small yellow throat and no external staining. It flowers very early and looks crisp against dark mulch or gravel.

planting.note: Plant in fall for gold blooms feathered with brown.

directions: Plant corms about two inches deep in gritty soil in a bright spot. The golden-yellow flowers are strongly feathered with purple-brown on the outer segments. An old, reliable snow crocus that increases well when left undisturbed.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn; naturalizes readily in lawns.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep beneath deciduous trees or in thin turf. This slender species self-sows and multiplies rapidly, forming sheets of soft lilac flowers over time. It is famously deer and rodent resistant and blooms very early in spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall for large reddish-purple early blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in well-drained soil in sun or dappled shade. The large reddish-violet flowers are more robust than the species and stand up well to weather. Excellent for naturalizing in grass, where it is largely ignored by squirrels.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for deep purple early blooms.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. This old selection carries deep purple flowers that are silvery on the outer petals. It naturalizes into charming colonies under shrubs and in short grass.

planting.note: Plant in fall for reddish-purple naturalizing blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in free-draining soil in light shade or sun. The flowers are a warm reddish-purple with paler silvery reverses, opening early in the season. It seeds around gently to build informal drifts.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for unusual rosy-toned blooms.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in well-drained soil in sun or partial shade. This form is prized for its rare rosy-pink lilac coloring, unusual among crocus. It is delicate but naturalizes slowly into small colonies in the early spring garden.

planting.note: Plant in fall for soft lilac early flowers.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty, well-drained ground. The slender flowers are a soft silvery lilac that opens to a warm orange stigma. Like others of its species it naturalizes freely and resists rodent damage.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall for reliable golden spring blooms.

directions: Set corms about three inches deep in well-drained soil and full sun. Also sold as Yellow Mammoth, this vigorous sterile hybrid produces bright golden-orange goblets in early to mid spring. It bulks up quickly and is one of the most dependable yellow crocus for naturalizing.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for bronze-backed golden blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in sharply drained soil in full sun. The rich golden-orange flowers are strongly striped mahogany-bronze on the outside and open flat like little stars. This old species does best where summers are dry and warm.

planting.note: Plant in fall for pale lilac blooms with gold throat.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun. The soft lilac-pink flowers have a bright golden-yellow throat that glows in early spring light. Compact and early, it is ideal for rock gardens, troughs, and the front of beds.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for three-banded flowers.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep in sharp-draining soil in a sunny spot. The flowers show three distinct bands, a lilac-purple tip, a white middle ring, and a golden-yellow throat. This striking selection is early and compact, perfect for alpine plantings.

planting.note: Plant in fall for white blooms with golden throat.

directions: Set corms two inches deep in gritty soil in full sun and good drainage. The pure white flowers have a rich golden-orange throat that lights up the early spring garden. It is compact, early, and excellent for rockeries and pots.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for soft lilac-blue blooms.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun. The soft lilac-blue flowers have a paler buff-veined exterior and a warm yellow throat. This robust selection of an Italian species naturalizes reliably in warm, dry sites.

planting.note: Plant in fall for buff-backed violet blooms.

directions: Set corms two to three inches deep in sharply drained soil in a warm, sunny position. The buds are buff-fawn feathered with purple on the outside and open to rich violet within. It is one of the earliest crocus, sometimes flowering in late winter.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn for bright yellow species blooms.

directions: Plant corms about three inches deep in free-draining soil and full sun. This wild species is the parent of many garden yellow crocus and bears warm orange-yellow flowers. It flowers in early spring and prefers a site that bakes dry in summer.
